message.content: im working in another session on astrolabe performance (turning briefs into specs). That process is slow and expensive, and is pretty wasteful since theres a hefty read that is essentially the same each time. Take a look at the status of that quest. Then, I'd like to explore some options which avoid the read by maintaining metadata about the project's shape in a way that can be conmsumed by the astrolabe without having to open/read all files or stuff a ton of content into the context. I know 'get shit done' framework maintains some metadata about a project. What is the actual purpose fo the reader step? What is it providing to the analyst? Can any of that be generated or primed by a non-LLm task, and/or calculated+cached+incrementally modified as needed?

input.command: nsg writ list --type quest --status open --limit 100 2>&1 | head -80

content.content:
[
  {
    "id": "w-mnyp279g-f565be10b053",
    "type": "quest",
    "status": "open",
    "title": "Tool-server MCP 500s drop mid-session on baseline astrolabe reader",
    "body": "<!-- Live body for this quest is a file in the vibers guild. See .claude/skills/quests/SKILL.md. -->\n<!-- Do not edit this row body while the quest is live. -->\n\n## Goal\n\nFind the root cause of a recurring failure mode in which a baseline astrolabe reader session starts with the MCP tool server healthy, successfully makes multiple MCP tool calls, and then mid-session every `inventory-write` (and other astrolabe tool) call starts returning HTTP 500 for the remainder of the session. The session's last-ditch retry loop burns several dollars and exits 0 without a usable inventory. We want to know *why the tool server stops responding mid-session*, not just defend against the symptom — this is distinct from the engine-precondition quest (`w-mny2ltvy`), which addresses the case where the tool is never reachable at start.",
    "createdAt": "2026-04-14T14:05:46.948Z",
    "updatedAt": "2026-04-14T14:05:46.948Z"
  },
  {
    "id": "w-mny2ltvy-e7016f61f768",
    "type": "quest",
    "status": "open",
    "title": "Engines should declare and verify MCP tool preconditions before starting sessions",
    "body": "<!-- Live body for this quest is a file in the vibers guild. See .claude/skills/quests/SKILL.md. -->\n<!-- Do not edit this row body while the quest is live. -->\n\n## Goal\n\nDecide and ship a design where agent-backed engine stages (notably astrolabe's anima-session stages — reader, spec-writer, and similar) declare the MCP tools their session depends on, and the runtime verifies those tools are actually reachable before starting the session. \"Reachable\" means more than \"the plugin is registered\" — it means the service backing the tool (e.g., a running guild daemon for tools that call the guild HTTP API) is alive and responding. Engines whose preconditions fail should refuse to start (or fail loudly with a precondition error) rather than spawning a session that will silently no-op its writes and still exit 0.",
    "createdAt": "2026-04-14T03:37:11.566Z",
    "updatedAt": "2026-04-14T03:37:11.566Z"
  },
  {
    "id": "w-mnxqv448-d63c8233b735",
    "type": "quest",
    "status": "open",
    "title": "Session consult — enter an existing autonomous session interactively",
    "body": "<!-- Live body for this quest is a file in the vibers guild. See .claude/skills/quests/SKILL.md. -->\n<!-- Do not edit this row body while the quest is live. -->\n\n## Goal\n\nDesign and ship the ability for the patron to \"enter\" a completed (or in-progress) autonomous session as an interactive multi-turn conversation — resuming the anima's full context window so the patron can ask questions, give corrections, or extend the work without starting from scratch. The outcome is a working `nsg consult --session <id>` (or equivalent) command backed by the Parlour apparatus.",
    "createdAt": "2026-04-13T22:08:29.336Z",
    "updatedAt": "2026-04-13T22:08:29.336Z"
  },
  {
    "id": "w-mnt3t5h8-943e2a2ef85f",
    "type": "quest",
    "status": "open",
    "title": "Astrolabe efficiency: reader/full-inventory cost reduction",
    "body": "<!-- Live body for this quest is a file in the vibers guild. See .claude/skills/quests/SKILL.md. -->\n<!-- Do not edit this row body while the quest is live. -->\n\n## Goal\n\nCut Astrolabe's end-to-end latency and token cost substantially, with the initial focus on the reader stage and its full-repository inventory pass — currently suspected to be the dominant contributor. The outcome is (a) a profile of where Astrolabe's time and tokens actually go across its stages, (b) a chosen set of interventions on the reader/inventory path (scoping, caching, incremental re-reads, hierarchical summarization, or similar), and (c) a sketch of any cross-stage changes that follow from the profiling (e.g., handoff format changes, dropping work the reader does that downstream stages don't use).",
    "createdAt": "2026-04-10T16:12:01.916Z",
    "updatedAt": "2026-04-13T21:36:06.960Z"
  },
  {
    "id": "w-mnt3r8al-b36b42b253b2",
    "type": "quest",
    "status": "open",
    "title": "Engine completion should be predicated on tool-call trace, not session exit",
    "body": "<!-- Live body for this quest is a file in the vibers guild. See .claude/skills/quests/SKILL.md. -->\n<!-- Do not edit this row body while the quest is live. -->\n\n## Goal\n\nDecide and then ship the design for making agent-backed engine stages (notably astrolabe's reader, spec-writer, and similar anima-session stages) treat \"success\" as a function of observable side effects — specifically, whether the required tool call actually landed in the session's tool-call trace — rather than trusting the session's clean exit and narrative summary. Outcome is a chosen defense stack (blame-point fix + retry loop + tool-call trace observability) ready to commission, with astrolabe pipeline contracts updated to match.",
    "createdAt": "2026-04-10T16:10:32.253Z",
    "updatedAt": "2026-04-13T21:36:06.960Z"
  },
  {
    "id": "w-mnt3di0m-22b4a5dac36a",
    "type": "quest",
    "status": "open",
    "title": "Daemon restart mid-summon strands engines in running state",
    "body": "<!-- Live body for this quest is a file in the vibers guild. See .claude/skills/quests/SKILL.md. -->\n<!-- Do not edit this row body while the quest is live. -->\n\n## Goal\n\nDecide how the spider should detect and recover from engines that were marked `running` but whose summon was interrupted by a daemon shutdown — and, separately, whether the daemon should shut down more gracefully in the first place so the problem doesn't arise as often. The outcome is a chosen design (one or a blend of reaping, atomicity, and graceful-shutdown approaches) ready to be commissioned as a mandate.",
    "createdAt": "2026-04-10T15:59:51.670Z",
    "updatedAt": "2026-04-13T21:36:06.959Z"
  },
  {
    "id": "w-mnt106rv-94bed1e0ace3",
    "type": "quest",
    "status": "open",
    "title": "Quest-helper CLI wrapper for nsg",
    "body": "<!-- Live body for this quest is a file in the vibers guild. See .claude/skills/quests/SKILL.md. -->\n<!-- Do not edit this row body while the quest is live. -->\n\n## Goal\n\nBuild a small CLI that wraps `nsg` with a quest-shaped interface for Coco (and other LLM agents working on quests). The wrapper encodes the file-canonical quest conventions as commands, so individual rituals (create, open for edit, close, migrate) can't drift or be executed out of order. This is a sanctum-side convenience layer, not a framework feature — it exists because the file-canonical quest model (parent quest w-mnt0jin1) is a convention layered on top of `nsg`, and discipline alone may not be enough to keep the convention consistent over time.",
    "parentId": "w-mnswvmj7-2112b86f710a",
    "createdAt": "2026-04-10T14:53:31.339Z",
    "updatedAt": "2026-04-13T21:36:06.959Z"
  },
  {
    "id": "w-mnt0i31e-1a1d46cb59a7",
    "type": "quest",
    "status": "open",
    "title": "Event-log layer for quest writs",
    "body": "<!-- Live body for this quest is a file in the vibers guild. See .claude/skills/quests/SKILL.md. -->\n<!-- Do not edit this row body while the quest is live. -->\n\n## Goal\n\nDecide the storage shape for quest-related events — status changes, child spawns, decisions ratified, edit messages, supersedes links. The narrative body of a quest synthesizes the *current picture*; the event log preserves the *timeline of how it got there*. These are different shapes wanting different storage. This quest is about picking the right one.",
    "parentId": "w-mnswvmj7-2112b86f710a",
    "createdAt": "2026-04-10T14:39:26.690Z",
    "updatedAt": "2026-04-13T21:36:06.959Z"
  },
  {
    "id": "w-mnszzon4-731bd9827d05",
    "type": "quest",
    "status": "open",
    "title": "Background monitoring of in-flight commissions (Vigil)",

content.content:
1	# Astrolabe efficiency: reader/full-inventory cost reduction
2	
3	## Goal
4	
5	Cut Astrolabe's end-to-end latency and token cost substantially, with the initial focus on the reader stage and its full-repository inventory pass — currently suspected to be the dominant contributor. The outcome is (a) a profile of where Astrolabe's time and tokens actually go across its stages, (b) a chosen set of interventions on the reader/inventory path (scoping, caching, incremental re-reads, hierarchical summarization, or similar), and (c) a sketch of any cross-stage changes that follow from the profiling (e.g., handoff format changes, dropping work the reader does that downstream stages don't use).
6	
7	## Status
8	
9	active — **all 6 rigs landed at `decision-review` 2026-04-14**; first-pass cost/timing analysis done; SSR hypothesis looks broken (not achieving predicted 10–20× reader reduction); MRA looks like the winner on cost and wall-clock; quality comparison still pending. Tool-dropout failure on baseline/MCP-precond recurred a second time, was recovered via DB edit, and spun out as its own quest (`w-mnyp279g-f565be10b053`).
10	
11	## Current batch (re-dispatched 2026-04-14)
12	
13	| Variant | Brief | Writ | Rig | Status |
14	|---|---|---|---|---|
15	| baseline (`brief`) | MCP-preconditions | `w-mnynxpwd-17535f30f61a` | `rig-mnynxqnu-798d4a34` | running |
16	| baseline (`brief`) | Prompt-injection | `w-mnynxfhj-2b38ea890673` | `rig-mnynxidw-fad80c71` | running |
17	| SSR (`brief-ssr`) | MCP-preconditions | `w-mnynxz7n-d658562985a6` | `rig-mnyny2tp-5ac56846` | running |
18	| SSR (`brief-ssr`) | Prompt-injection | `w-mnyny4bg-28b1974bb033` | `rig-mnyny78s-f7a83b39` | running |
19	| MRA (`brief-mra`) | MCP-preconditions | `w-mnynycgm-2200291d4972` | `rig-mnynyfjz-b9890309` | running |
20	| MRA (`brief-mra`) | Prompt-injection | `w-mnynyjub-a5398e15e8ea` | *(not yet started at table time)* | running |
21	
22	### Per-rig outcomes (as of 2026-04-14 mid-afternoon)
23	
24	- **`rig-mnynxqnu-798d4a34`** (baseline / MCP-preconditions) — Reader session `ses-mnynxr8e-500ec54d` burned $3.57 / 14min and narrated its own failure in prose after 6 `inventory-write` tool calls all returned HTTP 500. Second recurrence of this exact failure class on this exact writ. **Recovered** via transcript extraction + direct DB state-edit (see Recovery section); analyst is now running against the restored inventory.
25	- **5 other rigs** — ran clean through reader and analyst stages; blocked at `decision-review` awaiting patron.
26	- **Spun out quest `w-mnyp279g-f565be10b053`** to chase the root cause of the baseline-reader mid-session MCP 500s. That failure mode is **distinct** from the start-time precondition failure addressed by `w-mny2ltvy` — here the tool server was reachable at session start and the engine did real work before the transport died. If we find a fix, the baseline reader stops being fragile on this class of brief; if we don't, this is another point in favor of SSR/MRA (both variants ran clean on the identical writ body).
27	
28	### Recovery of rig-mnynxqnu (2026-04-14)
29	
30	When the second recurrence hit, Sean authorized recovery of the failing rig rather than throwing out the cell. Coco:
31	
32	1. Located the Claude Code transcript at `~/.claude/projects/-workspace-vibers-tmp-nexus-drafts-nexus-draft-mnynxr6a-10238a4a/f18786d3-cb5e-4074-9321-26415c7a8d82.jsonl` and extracted the 6 `inventory-write` tool-call inputs (sizes 15443 / 15663 / 12844 / 12424 / 5299 / 5343 chars — the decreasing size is itself interesting, suggests the model was shortening payloads thinking the size caused the 500s).
33	2. Picked attempt #2 (largest, 15796 chars) as the canonical inventory and wrote it with `nsg inventory-write --plan-id w-mnynxpwd-17535f30f61a --inventory ...`.
34	3. Backed up `nexus.db` and directly edited `books_spider_rigs` for `rig-mnynxqnu-798d4a34`: reset `inventory-check` → `pending`, reset downstream cascade-cancelled engines (`analyst` through `seal`) → `pending`, rig status → `running`. Spider picked up the change on next crawl, inventory-check re-ran against the recovered inventory, passed, analyst kicked off. Recovery clean.
35	
36	Caveat: the recovered inventory is the one the baseline reader *would have written* had the transport held — so when we do side-by-side quality comparison across variants, the baseline/MCP-preconditions cell reflects real reader output, not a fabrication. But it is not a pristine run end-to-end and should be flagged when reporting findings.
37	
38	## First-pass cost/timing analysis (2026-04-14)
39	
40	Per-session cost, duration, and cache-read for the reader and analyst phases of all 6 rigs. `n=1` per cell — effects are directional, not significant.
41	
42	| Variant | Brief | Stage | Cost | Duration | Cache read | Output |
43	|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
44	| baseline | MCP-precond | reader ⚠️ | $3.57 | 840s | 3.46M | 27.9k |
45	| baseline | MCP-precond | analyst (post-recovery) | $0.73 | 200s | 0.35M | 8.5k |
46	| baseline | prompt-inj | reader | $1.43 | 220s | 1.31M | 10.6k |
47	| baseline | prompt-inj | analyst | $0.88 | 240s | 0.40M | 10.3k |
48	| SSR | MCP-precond | reader | $2.66 | 305s | 3.25M | 11.7k |
49	| SSR | MCP-precond | analyst | $1.96 | 395s | 2.13M | 12.3k |
50	| SSR | prompt-inj | reader | $1.05 | 190s | 0.49M | 10.1k |
51	| SSR | prompt-inj | analyst | $1.08 | 310s | 0.76M | 13.4k |
52	| MRA | MCP-precond | reader-analyst | $2.35 | 500s | 2.05M | 19.7k |
53	| MRA | prompt-inj | reader-analyst | $1.66 | 345s | 1.43M | 16.2k |
54	
55	⚠️ = reader burned 6 retry attempts against tool-server 500s; cost/duration inflated vs clean run.
56	
57	**Per-brief totals (reader + analyst combined):**
58	
59	| Brief | baseline | SSR | MRA |
60	|---|---|---|---|
61	| MCP-precond | $4.30 / 1040s ⚠️ | $4.62 / 700s | **$2.35 / 500s** |
62	| prompt-inj  | $2.31 / 460s    | $2.13 / 500s  | **$1.66 / 345s** |
63	
64	### Findings vs hypotheses
65	
66	**H1: Reader dominated by turn count (~25).** The profile said baseline reader ~25 turns mean. Current measurement: baseline prompt-inj reader ran **86 assistant turns / 60 tool calls**, baseline MCP-precond reader 84 turns / 55 tool calls. Either the profile's "turn" was counted differently (tool-call *pairs*, or some other unit) or the reader has gotten chattier. Either way, our mental model of "~25 turns" is wrong for this batch — the baseline reader is doing ~3× that. Worth reconciling the measurement before treating the 25-turn number as a baseline for future planning.
67	
68	**H2: SSR single-shot should be 10–20× cheaper than baseline reader.** **Not confirmed.**
69	- Prompt-inj: SSR reader $1.05 / 190s vs baseline reader $1.43 / 220s — maybe 25% cheaper, possibly within noise.
70	- MCP-precond: SSR reader $2.66 / 305s vs baseline $3.57 / 840s (inflated). Removing the retry tail, clean baseline likely ~$1.50–2.00, which would make SSR *comparable or worse*.
71	- SSR reader cache-read is still in the millions (3.25M on MCP-precond, 0.49M on prompt-inj). That's not the shape of a 1–2 turn session — **SSR is not actually single-shotting**. Whatever the SSR prompt says, the model is going multi-turn in practice.
72	
73	This is the **headline negative finding**: SSR may not be doing what its name implies. The next investigation step is to read the actual SSR reader transcripts to see what it's really up to.
74	
75	**H3: MRA ≈ baseline analyst alone.** **Partially confirmed — MRA wins on cost in both briefs, but it's not quite "analyst alone."**
76	- Prompt-inj: MRA $1.66 vs baseline analyst $0.88 — MRA is ~2× the pure analyst cost, because it's also doing the reading. But $0.65 cheaper than baseline total ($2.31), $0.47 cheaper than SSR total ($2.13).
77	- MCP-precond: MRA $2.35, cheapest even against a clean-baseline correction.
78	- MRA is also the **fastest wall-clock** in both briefs. No reader↔analyst handoff latency.
79	
80	**H4: Brief-shape effects.** Bigger brief (MCP-precond, ~25 KB) runs slower and more expensive across all variants, but the *ranking* between variants is consistent across briefs. No variant flipped ordering by brief. The tool-dropout failure on baseline/MCP-precond is *not* a brief-shape effect — it's a distinct tool-server pathology (see `w-mnyp279g`).
81	
82	### Headline picture
83	
84	- **MRA is the provisional winner** on both cost and wall-clock across both briefs, by a meaningful margin.
85	- **SSR is a disappointment** — not collapsing turns the way intervention A predicted. May be rescuable with a prompt fix, may not. **This is the open investigation:** read SSR transcripts and figure out why it's still multi-turn.
86	- **Baseline carries a tail-risk** on specific writ content (2/2 tool-dropout failures on the same writ, different runs). That's a reliability finding even if the cost numbers are within striking distance.
87	- **Quality hasn't been compared yet** — if SSR or MRA produced meaningfully worse plans, cost wins don't matter. That's the gate on any promotion decision.
88	
89	## History — first batch (cancelled 2026-04-14)
90	
91	Astrolabe templates for SSR (`brief-ssr` → `planning-ssr`) and MRA (`brief-mra` → `planning-mra`) both shipped earlier and are in place. Draft-mandate flow wired up same session: registered `draft-mandate` writ type and set `astrolabe.generatedWritType = 'draft-mandate'` in `/workspace/vibers/guild.json` so astrolabe's `spec-publish` posts an inert writ instead of auto-dispatching to the artificer. `draft-mandate` is not in `spider.rigTemplateMappings`, so Spider ignores it. Unblocks A/B testing without burning implementation tokens.
92	
93	Two briefs drafted at `.scratch/brief-ab-mcp-preconditions.md` and `.scratch/brief-ab-prompt-injection-hardening.md`. First attempt at brief 2 was the daemon-restart-mid-summon quest (`w-mnt3di0m`); retracted because it overlapped with in-flight detached-sessions work (startup orphan sweep, SIGTERM handling, same file surfaces in `spider.ts` / `startup.ts`). Prompt-injection replaced it — clean non-overlapping code surface (clerk writ-post, commission-post CLI).
94	
95	First batch dispatched as 6 rigs across `brief` / `brief-ssr` / `brief-mra` × 2 briefs. Spider zombie reaper (removed in a parallel Coco session earlier the same day) had reaped 5 of 6 reader sessions mid-flight; Sean manually re-ran the reader stages, which meant all 5 re-ran against a likely-warm prompt cache — **contaminating the cost data**. One rig (`rig-mny49p49`, MCP-preconditions baseline) hit the exact failure shape its own brief targets: reader billed $2.61 / 10:40 against HTTP 500s from the MCP tool server, narrated its own failure in prose, exited 0. Captured as recurrence evidence on quest `w-mny2ltvy` — broadens the failure class from "dead daemon PID" to "any unreachable MCP tool backing service" and strengthens the case for precondition probing over retry tuning.
96	
97	Sean reversed course and **cancelled all tainted rigs** rather than salvaging quality data from contaminated runs. Six fresh writs reposted with `[EXP]` prefix (see table above).
98	
99	## Next Steps
100	
101	1. **Investigate why SSR is multi-turn.** Read the SSR reader transcripts and figure out what the model is actually doing with all those turns. Is it ignoring the single-shot instruction? Is the prompt permissive about "just verify" follow-ups? Is it coming from a structured tool-call pattern (e.g., exhaustive Read per file in the scope)? This is the decisive investigation for whether SSR can be rescued or should be retired.
102	2. **Compare the 6 plan docs side-by-side** for quality: inventory completeness, scope/decisions/observations shape, any gaps SSR or MRA introduced versus baseline. This gates any promotion decision.
103	3. **Reconcile the "~25 turns" profile number** — figure out if that was a different turn definition or if the reader has actually gotten chattier since the profile. Affects any future planning that leans on that number.
104	4. **Decide**: promote MRA to default, keep SSR as a secondary option if quality is good, or retire SSR if it's both expensive and not distinctively better.
105	5. **Second-order target once reader is handled**: `implement` (artificer rig) at $183 / 90 runs.
106	
107	## Context
108	
109	**2026-04-10 — Profile complete.** Full findings in `experiments/data/2026-04-10-astrolabe-profile.md`. Key numbers:
110	
111	- Astrolabe spend captured: **$107.35** across 51 sessions (22 reader, 17 analyst, 12 spec-writer) in the guild books.
112	- **Reader is 65% of astrolabe's spend** ($69.75). Mean $3.17/session, max $8.22, 5.2× spread.
113	- **Reader's cost is dominated by turn count, not load size.** Mean ~25 turns per session, each re-reading its accumulated ~107 k-token cache. The cheapest run loaded 66 k and took 24 turns; the most expensive loaded 118 k and took 26 turns but cost 5× more — turn-count multiplier dominates.
114	- Reader produces a **well-bounded, structured output** (~17 k tokens, consistent sections every time). The agent already knows the deliverable shape — it's spending 25 turns getting there via filesystem poking.
115	- Conversation-cache-sharing between reader and downstream saves only ~27% on downstream cost, much less than expected. Downstream stages still load ~1.3 M cache-read of their own material. Suggests reader may be partially redundant with analyst.
116	- 2/22 reader sessions exit-failed at ~$2.40 each. One session silently ran in a degraded mode because its output tools (plan-show, inventory-write) weren't available — it wrote a file to the worktree and still billed $2.26. Separate observability gap worth a dedicated quest.
117	
118	**Intervention ranking from the profile:**
119	
120	- **A. Single-shot reader prompt** (highest leverage) — collapse ~25 turns → 1–2. Estimated 10–20× cost reduction. Dispatched as a commission.
121	- **B. Merge reader into analyst** — removes 65% of spend; bigger design risk but bigger upside if it holds quality. Dispatched as a commission.
122	- **C. Shrink per-turn cache-read** — 20–40% savings; order of magnitude less leverage than A. Not pursued yet.
123	- **D. Fail-fast on tool-availability** — hygiene/correctness, not cost. Candidate for its own quest.
124	- **E. Retry failed readers** — not pursuing; root-cause fix via A/B addresses it.
125	
126	A and B were dispatched concurrently as experimental secondary rig templates so production astrolabe (`brief` → `planning`) stays untouched while we A/B test. Each experimental variant gets its own writ type so commissions route to the right template without any global flag.
127	
128	**Related earlier thinking:** the Unified instrument context for cache efficiency quest attacks a similar shape of problem on the instrument side (shared prefix, many suffixes). Cost-tracking work on Coinmaster/Purse/Tithe is the measurement side — once that exists, profiling becomes continuous rather than one-off.
129	
130	## References
131	
132	- **`experiments/data/2026-04-10-astrolabe-profile.md`** — full profile findings (51-session cohort, intervention ranking, recommended experimental design).
133	- `/workspace/nexus/packages/plugins/astrolabe/src/astrolabe.ts` — framework source; the `planningTemplate` rig template and `rigTemplateMappings` block are where the experimental variants hook in.
134	- `/workspace/nexus/packages/plugins/astrolabe/src/engines/` — existing engine implementations.
135	- Commission briefs: `.scratch/brief-astrolabe-ssr-template.md` (w-mnt966d8), `.scratch/brief-astrolabe-mra-template.md` (w-mnt968x4).
136	- Related quest: **Unified instrument context for cache efficiency** — prompt-prefix sharing trick for instrument runs; may generalize to astrolabe stages.
137	- Related quest: **Cost tracking & token budget allocation (Coinmaster/Purse/Tithe)** `w-mnszznt5-66d0ec7464bc`.
138	- Related quest: **Engine completion should be predicated on tool-call trace, not session exit** `w-mnt3r8al-b36b42b253b2`.
139	- Guild vocabulary: `docs/future/guild-vocabulary.md`.
140	
141	## Notes
142	
143	- (2026-04-10) Sean: "increase astrolabe efficiency. it is very slow and costly right now, and i think the reader/full inventory is a big part of that"
144	- (2026-04-10) Profile confirmed Sean's instinct — reader is the dominant cost, but the surprising finding is that the dominance comes from *turn count* not *load size*. This reframes the intervention space: caching/scoping wins much less than prompt restructuring.
145	- (2026-04-10) Observability gap flagged but not yet captured as its own quest: reader silently degrades when output tools aren't available. Create follow-up quest if the single-shot work doesn't incidentally address it.

# Astrolabe efficiency: reader/full-inventory cost reduction

## Goal

Cut Astrolabe's end-to-end latency and token cost substantially, with the initial focus on the reader stage and its full-repository inventory pass — currently suspected to be the dominant contributor. The outcome is (a) a profile of where Astrolabe's time and tokens actually go across its stages, (b) a chosen set of interventions on the reader/inventory path (scoping, caching, incremental re-reads, hierarchical summarization, or similar), and (c) a sketch of any cross-stage changes that follow from the profiling (e.g., handoff format changes, dropping work the reader does that downstream stages don't use).

## Status

active — **all 6 rigs landed at `decision-review` 2026-04-14**; first-pass cost/timing analysis done; SSR hypothesis looks broken (not achieving predicted 10–20× reader reduction); MRA looks like the winner on cost and wall-clock; quality comparison still pending. Tool-dropout failure on baseline/MCP-precond recurred a second time, was recovered via DB edit, and spun out as its own quest (`w-mnyp279g-f565be10b053`).

## Current batch (re-dispatched 2026-04-14)

| Variant | Brief | Writ | Rig | Status |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| baseline (`brief`) | MCP-preconditions | `w-mnynxpwd-17535f30f61a` | `rig-mnynxqnu-798d4a34` | running |
| baseline (`brief`) | Prompt-injection | `w-mnynxfhj-2b38ea890673` | `rig-mnynxidw-fad80c71` | running |
| SSR (`brief-ssr`) | MCP-preconditions | `w-mnynxz7n-d658562985a6` | `rig-mnyny2tp-5ac56846` | running |
| SSR (`brief-ssr`) | Prompt-injection | `w-mnyny4bg-28b1974bb033` | `rig-mnyny78s-f7a83b39` | running |
| MRA (`brief-mra`) | MCP-preconditions | `w-mnynycgm-2200291d4972` | `rig-mnynyfjz-b9890309` | running |
| MRA (`brief-mra`) | Prompt-injection | `w-mnynyjub-a5398e15e8ea` | *(not yet started at table time)* | running |

### Per-rig outcomes (as of 2026-04-14 mid-afternoon)

- **`rig-mnynxqnu-798d4a34`** (baseline / MCP-preconditions) — Reader session `ses-mnynxr8e-500ec54d` burned $3.57 / 14min and narrated its own failure in prose after 6 `inventory-write` tool calls all returned HTTP 500. Second recurrence of this exact failure class on this exact writ. **Recovered** via transcript extraction + direct DB state-edit (see Recovery section); analyst is now running against the restored inventory.
- **5 other rigs** — ran clean through reader and analyst stages; blocked at `decision-review` awaiting patron.
- **Spun out quest `w-mnyp279g-f565be10b053`** to chase the root cause of the baseline-reader mid-session MCP 500s. That failure mode is **distinct** from the start-time precondition failure addressed by `w-mny2ltvy` — here the tool server was reachable at session start and the engine did real work before the transport died. If we find a fix, the baseline reader stops being fragile on this class of brief; if we don't, this is another point in favor of SSR/MRA (both variants ran clean on the identical writ body).

### Recovery of rig-mnynxqnu (2026-04-14)

When the second recurrence hit, Sean authorized recovery of the failing rig rather than throwing out the cell. Coco:

1. Located the Claude Code transcript at `~/.claude/projects/-workspace-vibers-tmp-nexus-drafts-nexus-draft-mnynxr6a-10238a4a/f18786d3-cb5e-4074-9321-26415c7a8d82.jsonl` and extracted the 6 `inventory-write` tool-call inputs (sizes 15443 / 15663 / 12844 / 12424 / 5299 / 5343 chars — the decreasing size is itself interesting, suggests the model was shortening payloads thinking the size caused the 500s).
2. Picked attempt #2 (largest, 15796 chars) as the canonical inventory and wrote it with `nsg inventory-write --plan-id w-mnynxpwd-17535f30f61a --inventory ...`.
3. Backed up `nexus.db` and directly edited `books_spider_rigs` for `rig-mnynxqnu-798d4a34`: reset `inventory-check` → `pending`, reset downstream cascade-cancelled engines (`analyst` through `seal`) → `pending`, rig status → `running`. Spider picked up the change on next crawl, inventory-check re-ran against the recovered inventory, passed, analyst kicked off. Recovery clean.

Caveat: the recovered inventory is the one the baseline reader *would have written* had the transport held — so when we do side-by-side quality comparison across variants, the baseline/MCP-preconditions cell reflects real reader output, not a fabrication. But it is not a pristine run end-to-end and should be flagged when reporting findings.

## First-pass cost/timing analysis (2026-04-14)

Per-session cost, duration, and cache-read for the reader and analyst phases of all 6 rigs. `n=1` per cell — effects are directional, not significant.

| Variant | Brief | Stage | Cost | Duration | Cache read | Output |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| baseline | MCP-precond | reader ⚠️ | $3.57 | 840s | 3.46M | 27.9k |
| baseline | MCP-precond | analyst (post-recovery) | $0.73 | 200s | 0.35M | 8.5k |
| baseline | prompt-inj | reader | $1.43 | 220s | 1.31M | 10.6k |
| baseline | prompt-inj | analyst | $0.88 | 240s | 0.40M | 10.3k |
| SSR | MCP-precond | reader | $2.66 | 305s | 3.25M | 11.7k |
| SSR | MCP-precond | analyst | $1.96 | 395s | 2.13M | 12.3k |
| SSR | prompt-inj | reader | $1.05 | 190s | 0.49M | 10.1k |
| SSR | prompt-inj | analyst | $1.08 | 310s | 0.76M | 13.4k |
| MRA | MCP-precond | reader-analyst | $2.35 | 500s | 2.05M | 19.7k |
| MRA | prompt-inj | reader-analyst | $1.66 | 345s | 1.43M | 16.2k |

⚠️ = reader burned 6 retry attempts against tool-server 500s; cost/duration inflated vs clean run.

**Per-brief totals (reader + analyst combined):**

| Brief | baseline | SSR | MRA |
|---|---|---|---|
| MCP-precond | $4.30 / 1040s ⚠️ | $4.62 / 700s | **$2.35 / 500s** |
| prompt-inj  | $2.31 / 460s    | $2.13 / 500s  | **$1.66 / 345s** |

### Findings vs hypotheses

**H1: Reader dominated by turn count (~25).** The profile said baseline reader ~25 turns mean. Current measurement: baseline prompt-inj reader ran **86 assistant turns / 60 tool calls**, baseline MCP-precond reader 84 turns / 55 tool calls. Either the profile's "turn" was counted differently (tool-call *pairs*, or some other unit) or the reader has gotten chattier. Either way, our mental model of "~25 turns" is wrong for this batch — the baseline reader is doing ~3× that. Worth reconciling the measurement before treating the 25-turn number as a baseline for future planning.

**H2: SSR single-shot should be 10–20× cheaper than baseline reader.** **Not confirmed.**
- Prompt-inj: SSR reader $1.05 / 190s vs baseline reader $1.43 / 220s — maybe 25% cheaper, possibly within noise.
- MCP-precond: SSR reader $2.66 / 305s vs baseline $3.57 / 840s (inflated). Removing the retry tail, clean baseline likely ~$1.50–2.00, which would make SSR *comparable or worse*.
- SSR reader cache-read is still in the millions (3.25M on MCP-precond, 0.49M on prompt-inj). That's not the shape of a 1–2 turn session — **SSR is not actually single-shotting**. Whatever the SSR prompt says, the model is going multi-turn in practice.

This is the **headline negative finding**: SSR may not be doing what its name implies. The next investigation step is to read the actual SSR reader transcripts to see what it's really up to.

**H3: MRA ≈ baseline analyst alone.** **Partially confirmed — MRA wins on cost in both briefs, but it's not quite "analyst alone."**
- Prompt-inj: MRA $1.66 vs baseline analyst $0.88 — MRA is ~2× the pure analyst cost, because it's also doing the reading. But $0.65 cheaper than baseline total ($2.31), $0.47 cheaper than SSR total ($2.13).
- MCP-precond: MRA $2.35, cheapest even against a clean-baseline correction.
- MRA is also the **fastest wall-clock** in both briefs. No reader↔analyst handoff latency.

**H4: Brief-shape effects.** Bigger brief (MCP-precond, ~25 KB) runs slower and more expensive across all variants, but the *ranking* between variants is consistent across briefs. No variant flipped ordering by brief. The tool-dropout failure on baseline/MCP-precond is *not* a brief-shape effect — it's a distinct tool-server pathology (see `w-mnyp279g`).

### Headline picture

- **MRA is the provisional winner** on both cost and wall-clock across both briefs, by a meaningful margin.
- **SSR is a disappointment** — not collapsing turns the way intervention A predicted. May be rescuable with a prompt fix, may not. **This is the open investigation:** read SSR transcripts and figure out why it's still multi-turn.
- **Baseline carries a tail-risk** on specific writ content (2/2 tool-dropout failures on the same writ, different runs). That's a reliability finding even if the cost numbers are within striking distance.
- **Quality hasn't been compared yet** — if SSR or MRA produced meaningfully worse plans, cost wins don't matter. That's the gate on any promotion decision.

## History — first batch (cancelled 2026-04-14)

Astrolabe templates for SSR (`brief-ssr` → `planning-ssr`) and MRA (`brief-mra` → `planning-mra`) both shipped earlier and are in place. Draft-mandate flow wired up same session: registered `draft-mandate` writ type and set `astrolabe.generatedWritType = 'draft-mandate'` in `/workspace/vibers/guild.json` so astrolabe's `spec-publish` posts an inert writ instead of auto-dispatching to the artificer. `draft-mandate` is not in `spider.rigTemplateMappings`, so Spider ignores it. Unblocks A/B testing without burning implementation tokens.

Two briefs drafted at `.scratch/brief-ab-mcp-preconditions.md` and `.scratch/brief-ab-prompt-injection-hardening.md`. First attempt at brief 2 was the daemon-restart-mid-summon quest (`w-mnt3di0m`); retracted because it overlapped with in-flight detached-sessions work (startup orphan sweep, SIGTERM handling, same file surfaces in `spider.ts` / `startup.ts`). Prompt-injection replaced it — clean non-overlapping code surface (clerk writ-post, commission-post CLI).

First batch dispatched as 6 rigs across `brief` / `brief-ssr` / `brief-mra` × 2 briefs. Spider zombie reaper (removed in a parallel Coco session earlier the same day) had reaped 5 of 6 reader sessions mid-flight; Sean manually re-ran the reader stages, which meant all 5 re-ran against a likely-warm prompt cache — **contaminating the cost data**. One rig (`rig-mny49p49`, MCP-preconditions baseline) hit the exact failure shape its own brief targets: reader billed $2.61 / 10:40 against HTTP 500s from the MCP tool server, narrated its own failure in prose, exited 0. Captured as recurrence evidence on quest `w-mny2ltvy` — broadens the failure class from "dead daemon PID" to "any unreachable MCP tool backing service" and strengthens the case for precondition probing over retry tuning.

Sean reversed course and **cancelled all tainted rigs** rather than salvaging quality data from contaminated runs. Six fresh writs reposted with `[EXP]` prefix (see table above).

## Next Steps

1. **Investigate why SSR is multi-turn.** Read the SSR reader transcripts and figure out what the model is actually doing with all those turns. Is it ignoring the single-shot instruction? Is the prompt permissive about "just verify" follow-ups? Is it coming from a structured tool-call pattern (e.g., exhaustive Read per file in the scope)? This is the decisive investigation for whether SSR can be rescued or should be retired.
2. **Compare the 6 plan docs side-by-side** for quality: inventory completeness, scope/decisions/observations shape, any gaps SSR or MRA introduced versus baseline. This gates any promotion decision.
3. **Reconcile the "~25 turns" profile number** — figure out if that was a different turn definition or if the reader has actually gotten chattier since the profile. Affects any future planning that leans on that number.
4. **Decide**: promote MRA to default, keep SSR as a secondary option if quality is good, or retire SSR if it's both expensive and not distinctively better.
5. **Second-order target once reader is handled**: `implement` (artificer rig) at $183 / 90 runs.

## Context

**2026-04-10 — Profile complete.** Full findings in `experiments/data/2026-04-10-astrolabe-profile.md`. Key numbers:

- Astrolabe spend captured: **$107.35** across 51 sessions (22 reader, 17 analyst, 12 spec-writer) in the guild books.
- **Reader is 65% of astrolabe's spend** ($69.75). Mean $3.17/session, max $8.22, 5.2× spread.
- **Reader's cost is dominated by turn count, not load size.** Mean ~25 turns per session, each re-reading its accumulated ~107 k-token cache. The cheapest run loaded 66 k and took 24 turns; the most expensive loaded 118 k and took 26 turns but cost 5× more — turn-count multiplier dominates.
- Reader produces a **well-bounded, structured output** (~17 k tokens, consistent sections every time). The agent already knows the deliverable shape — it's spending 25 turns getting there via filesystem poking.
- Conversation-cache-sharing between reader and downstream saves only ~27% on downstream cost, much less than expected. Downstream stages still load ~1.3 M cache-read of their own material. Suggests reader may be partially redundant with analyst.
- 2/22 reader sessions exit-failed at ~$2.40 each. One session silently ran in a degraded mode because its output tools (plan-show, inventory-write) weren't available — it wrote a file to the worktree and still billed $2.26. Separate observability gap worth a dedicated quest.

**Intervention ranking from the profile:**

- **A. Single-shot reader prompt** (highest leverage) — collapse ~25 turns → 1–2. Estimated 10–20× cost reduction. Dispatched as a commission.
- **B. Merge reader into analyst** — removes 65% of spend; bigger design risk but bigger upside if it holds quality. Dispatched as a commission.
- **C. Shrink per-turn cache-read** — 20–40% savings; order of magnitude less leverage than A. Not pursued yet.
- **D. Fail-fast on tool-availability** — hygiene/correctness, not cost. Candidate for its own quest.
- **E. Retry failed readers** — not pursuing; root-cause fix via A/B addresses it.

A and B were dispatched concurrently as experimental secondary rig templates so production astrolabe (`brief` → `planning`) stays untouched while we A/B test. Each experimental variant gets its own writ type so commissions route to the right template without any global flag.

**Related earlier thinking:** the Unified instrument context for cache efficiency quest attacks a similar shape of problem on the instrument side (shared prefix, many suffixes). Cost-tracking work on Coinmaster/Purse/Tithe is the measurement side — once that exists, profiling becomes continuous rather than one-off.

## References

- **`experiments/data/2026-04-10-astrolabe-profile.md`** — full profile findings (51-session cohort, intervention ranking, recommended experimental design).
- `/workspace/nexus/packages/plugins/astrolabe/src/astrolabe.ts` — framework source; the `planningTemplate` rig template and `rigTemplateMappings` block are where the experimental variants hook in.
- `/workspace/nexus/packages/plugins/astrolabe/src/engines/` — existing engine implementations.
- Commission briefs: `.scratch/brief-astrolabe-ssr-template.md` (w-mnt966d8), `.scratch/brief-astrolabe-mra-template.md` (w-mnt968x4).
- Related quest: **Unified instrument context for cache efficiency** — prompt-prefix sharing trick for instrument runs; may generalize to astrolabe stages.
- Related quest: **Cost tracking & token budget allocation (Coinmaster/Purse/Tithe)** `w-mnszznt5-66d0ec7464bc`.
- Related quest: **Engine completion should be predicated on tool-call trace, not session exit** `w-mnt3r8al-b36b42b253b2`.
- Guild vocabulary: `docs/future/guild-vocabulary.md`.

## Notes

- (2026-04-10) Sean: "increase astrolabe efficiency. it is very slow and costly right now, and i think the reader/full inventory is a big part of that"
- (2026-04-10) Profile confirmed Sean's instinct — reader is the dominant cost, but the surprising finding is that the dominance comes from *turn count* not *load size*. This reframes the intervention space: caching/scoping wins much less than prompt restructuring.
- (2026-04-10) Observability gap flagged but not yet captured as its own quest: reader silently degrades when output tools aren't available. Create follow-up quest if the single-shot work doesn't incidentally address it.

7:**Reader is 65% of astrolabe's total spend, and its cost is dominated by turn count — not by how much context it loads.** The average reader session re-reads its accumulated cache **~25 times** before finishing. Caching or scoping the *input* to reader will help less than reducing the *number of turns* it takes to produce its inventory. A single-shot or two-shot reader prompt is the highest-leverage intervention.
50:This reframes the intervention space. If reader's expense were *fresh reading*, caching and scoping would win. Because it's *re-reading the same cache 25 times*, the winning move is to **reduce turns** — collapse the scan into a single or two-phase prompt that emits a structured inventory without iterative exploration.
54:Reading the `output` field of every reader session: they all emit the same shape — a markdown inventory with consistent sections:
65:[Omitted long matching line]
94:Re-frame reader as "produce this structured inventory in one response" rather than "explore the codebase and then emit a report." Give the agent the brief, the file tree, and a strict output template. Allow one follow-up turn for clarification reads if needed.
105:- **Risk:** loses the separation-of-concerns pattern that the astrolabe rig relies on for checkpointing. The plan doc currently has an inventory-then-analysis handoff that makes recovery clean.
119:- **Expected impact:** small — maybe $2/failure × 1 failure per ~20 runs = ~$0.10 per writ average. Not a cost play, but a correctness / data-quality play (bad reader outputs poison downstream).
130:- **Per-turn token attribution.** Would let us isolate whether early turns (scanning) or late turns (writing inventory) dominate.
131:- **Actual handoff consumption.** Can't prove downstream isn't consuming reader output without reading analyst/spec-writer transcripts and checking what content they reference.

content.content:
54	Reading the `output` field of every reader session: they all emit the same shape — a markdown inventory with consistent sections:
55	
56	- **Brief summary** (1–2 sentences)
57	- **Primary files to modify** (annotated file list, typically 3–6 entries)
58	- **Key findings** (3–6 numbered bullets identifying the gap, critical prerequisite bugs, circular/race risks)
59	- **Test files** (paths and pattern notes)
60	- **Adjacent patterns** (code references the implementer will want to mirror)
61	- **Doc/code discrepancies** (optional, often 3–6 items)
62	
63	This is a **well-bounded, structured output**. Every reader session is producing a document of roughly the same shape, ~17 k tokens ± 50%. There is no open-ended exploration stage — the agent knows what it's looking for by the end. But it's getting there via ~25 turns of filesystem poking.
64	
65	One session in the sample emitted a telling note: the `plan-show` and `inventory-write` tools were not available in the Claude Code session, so the agent could not read the brief writ or write to the PlanDoc. It fell back to writing a file into the worktree and committing it — and still billed $2.26 and 545 s. This is a separate finding (observability gap: tool-availability failures silently degrade reader to a less useful mode without failing the stage), but it underscores that **reader's 25-turn exploration is not contingent on finding the right information** — it happens even when reader's own output path is broken.
66	
67	## 5. Does downstream reuse reader's work?
68	
69	In shared-conversation runs, downstream stages inherit reader's prompt cache. I split the 17 multi-stage writs by whether analyst/spec-writer shared reader's conversation:
70	
71	| pattern | writs | avg reader $ | avg analyst $ | avg spec-writer $ |
72	|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|
73	| shared conv (reader cache reused) | 9 | $3.86 | $1.06 | $1.12 |
74	| separate conv (reader is discarded) | 8 | $2.85 | $1.45 | $0.80 |
75	
76	Shared-conv reader sessions are ~35% more expensive on average (likely because those writs are larger, hence the decision to share), but downstream analyst sessions **are not dramatically cheaper** — $1.06 vs $1.45, a 27% reduction. That is much less savings than you'd expect if analyst were riding reader's cache for a free lift. Possible explanations:
77	
78	- Claude Code's session handoff loses most of the cache benefit across session boundaries even with the same `conversationId`.
79	- Analyst is loading substantially different material than reader (consistent with analyst doing focused decision-point analysis rather than scanning).
80	- Analyst is still doing its own multi-turn exploration and paying the same cache-read tax that reader pays.
81	
82	The 27% delta is not enough to justify the shared-conv complexity as a primary optimization lever. **Cutting reader cost directly is the higher-leverage play than trying to improve handoff efficiency.**
83	
84	## 6. Cross-stage handoff: what analyst/spec-writer don't need
85	
86	Analyst sessions average **1.44 M cacheRead** and **11.3 k output**. Spec-writer averages **1.31 M cacheRead** and **11.9 k output**. Both are doing substantial multi-turn work themselves on material that *may or may not* overlap with what reader loaded. Without a tool-call trace I can't prove reader is producing unused material, but the fact that downstream stages load ~1.3 M of their own cache *on top of* any inherited reader cache strongly suggests **downstream is re-reading source material directly, not consuming reader's structured output.**
87	
88	If that's true, the most efficient world may not include reader at all — or reader reduced to a single-shot *directory structure + entry-point identification* step that saves the other stages navigation time without trying to pre-digest findings for them.
89	
90	## 7. Intervention shortlist (ranked by estimated impact)
91	
92	### A. Single-shot or two-shot reader prompt *(highest leverage)*
93

file.content:
Reading the `output` field of every reader session: they all emit the same shape — a markdown inventory with consistent sections:

- **Brief summary** (1–2 sentences)
- **Primary files to modify** (annotated file list, typically 3–6 entries)
- **Key findings** (3–6 numbered bullets identifying the gap, critical prerequisite bugs, circular/race risks)
- **Test files** (paths and pattern notes)
- **Adjacent patterns** (code references the implementer will want to mirror)
- **Doc/code discrepancies** (optional, often 3–6 items)

This is a **well-bounded, structured output**. Every reader session is producing a document of roughly the same shape, ~17 k tokens ± 50%. There is no open-ended exploration stage — the agent knows what it's looking for by the end. But it's getting there via ~25 turns of filesystem poking.

One session in the sample emitted a telling note: the `plan-show` and `inventory-write` tools were not available in the Claude Code session, so the agent could not read the brief writ or write to the PlanDoc. It fell back to writing a file into the worktree and committing it — and still billed $2.26 and 545 s. This is a separate finding (observability gap: tool-availability failures silently degrade reader to a less useful mode without failing the stage), but it underscores that **reader's 25-turn exploration is not contingent on finding the right information** — it happens even when reader's own output path is broken.

## 5. Does downstream reuse reader's work?

In shared-conversation runs, downstream stages inherit reader's prompt cache. I split the 17 multi-stage writs by whether analyst/spec-writer shared reader's conversation:

| pattern | writs | avg reader $ | avg analyst $ | avg spec-writer $ |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| shared conv (reader cache reused) | 9 | $3.86 | $1.06 | $1.12 |
| separate conv (reader is discarded) | 8 | $2.85 | $1.45 | $0.80 |

Shared-conv reader sessions are ~35% more expensive on average (likely because those writs are larger, hence the decision to share), but downstream analyst sessions **are not dramatically cheaper** — $1.06 vs $1.45, a 27% reduction. That is much less savings than you'd expect if analyst were riding reader's cache for a free lift. Possible explanations:

- Claude Code's session handoff loses most of the cache benefit across session boundaries even with the same `conversationId`.
- Analyst is loading substantially different material than reader (consistent with analyst doing focused decision-point analysis rather than scanning).
- Analyst is still doing its own multi-turn exploration and paying the same cache-read tax that reader pays.

The 27% delta is not enough to justify the shared-conv complexity as a primary optimization lever. **Cutting reader cost directly is the higher-leverage play than trying to improve handoff efficiency.**

## 6. Cross-stage handoff: what analyst/spec-writer don't need

Analyst sessions average **1.44 M cacheRead** and **11.3 k output**. Spec-writer averages **1.31 M cacheRead** and **11.9 k output**. Both are doing substantial multi-turn work themselves on material that *may or may not* overlap with what reader loaded. Without a tool-call trace I can't prove reader is producing unused material, but the fact that downstream stages load ~1.3 M of their own cache *on top of* any inherited reader cache strongly suggests **downstream is re-reading source material directly, not consuming reader's structured output.**

If that's true, the most efficient world may not include reader at all — or reader reduced to a single-shot *directory structure + entry-point identification* step that saves the other stages navigation time without trying to pre-digest findings for them.

## 7. Intervention shortlist (ranked by estimated impact)

### A. Single-shot or two-shot reader prompt *(highest leverage)*

content.text:
Pulled up the quest and the 2026-04-10 profile. Here's where we are and then the analytical work on your question.

## Quest status — `w-mnt3t5h8` (Astrolabe efficiency)

**All 6 A/B rigs landed at `decision-review` today.** First-pass cost/timing is in; quality comparison still pending.

Per-brief totals (reader+analyst):

| Brief | baseline | SSR | MRA |
|---|---|---|---|
| MCP-precond | $4.30 / 1040s ⚠️ | $4.62 / 700s | **$2.35 / 500s** |
| prompt-inj | $2.31 / 460s | $2.13 / 500s | **$1.66 / 345s** |

**Headline findings:**
- **MRA wins** on both cost and wall-clock across both briefs.
- **SSR is a disappointment.** It was supposed to collapse ~25 turns → 1–2 and deliver a 10–20× reader cost reduction. Instead its reader is *still multi-turn* (3.25M cache-read on MCP-precond — that's not the shape of a single-shot session). The next investigation step on that quest is reading the SSR transcripts to see what the model is actually doing with all those turns.
- **Baseline had another tool-dropout failure** on the MCP-precond writ; recovered via transcript extract + DB edit; spun out as `w-mnyp279g`.

Your new question dovetails with the SSR finding in a really important way — read on.

## Part 1: What is the reader actually *for*?

From the profile (section 4, 5, 6) plus the rig template, the reader's job — as currently scoped — is **brief-specific code localization and risk surfacing**, not "project structure extraction." Its deliverable is a structured markdown doc with six consistent sections:

1. Brief summary (restate in 1–2 sentences)
2. Primary files to modify (annotated, 3–6 entries)
3. Key findings (the gap, prereq bugs, race/circular risks)
4. Test files
5. Adjacent patterns (code to mirror)
6. Doc/code discrepancies

Notice what this is and isn't:
- It **is**: a retrieval + ranking + risk-annotation pass conditioned on the brief.
- It **is not**: a map of the project. It's a *brief-targeted slice* of the project.

And here's the kicker from profile section 6 that reframes everything:

> Analyst averages 1.44M cacheRead / 11.3k output. Spec-writer averages 1.31M / 11.9k. Both are doing substantial multi-turn work themselves on material that *may or may not* overlap with what reader loaded… downstream is re-reading source material directly, not consuming reader's structured output.

Handoff savings between reader and analyst were ~27%, not the ~70–90% you'd expect if analyst were actually riding on reader's work. **So reader's structured output is mostly landfill.** Analyst uses the conversation cache a little, but then goes and re-reads the code for itself. Reader is paying $3/run to produce a document that nobody really reads.

That reframes the whole optimization question. The reader isn't just slow — it's slow at a thing that isn't even load-bearing downstream. Which is why MRA is winning the A/B: merging reader into analyst deletes ~half the wasted work outright.

## Part 2: Where does the cost actually live?

From the profile: reader cost is **dominated by turn count, not load size**. ~25 turns per session, each re-reading the accumulated ~107k-token cache. The model is doing a `grep`/`Read`/`Glob` dance over the filesystem, building up a mental model it then writes out.

The LLM is effectively being used as:
- a **symbol index** (find functions, classes, call sites)
- a **dependency graph** (what imports what)
- a **test-file mapper** (which test covers which source)
- a **retrieval engine** (which files relate to these brief nouns)
- **and then**, on top, an actual analyst (what's risky, what's adjacent, what's a gotcha)

The first four are things non-LLM tooling does *faster, cheaper, and more accurately than an LLM ever will*. Only the last one genuinely needs a model.

## Part 3: Can we pre-compute the project shape?

**Yes, and there's well-trodden prior art.** The thing you half-remember from "Get Shit Done" — I'm genuinely not sure which project-shape metadata you're thinking of; GSD as a productivity method is task-level, but there may be a tool under that name I don't know. Can you point me at it? That said, the general space:

- **aider's repo-map** — builds a tree-sitter symbol index across the repo, weights symbols by PageRank-ish reference counts, and fits a budget-sized summary into the model's prompt. Essentially exactly the thing you're describing. It's incremental and cached.
- **Sourcegraph / Cody** — full code intel index (symbols, xrefs, definitions).
- **Cursor / Continue.dev** — embedding-based chunk index for semantic retrieval over code, plus symbol/AST views.
- **Classic**: `ctags`, `cscope`, `tree-sitter` directly.
- **Build-system metadata**: `tsc --listFiles`, `madge`, `dependency-cruiser`, Rust's `cargo metadata`, Nx's project graph.

The interesting separation for Astrolabe is:

**Tier A — static, brief-independent, trivially cacheable/incremental:**
- File tree with sizes, types, mtimes
- Symbol index per file (functions, classes, exports)
- Module dependency graph
- Test-file ↔ source-file mapping (by heuristic or build-system knowledge)
- Recent git log touching each file
- Convention/config fingerprint (linter, formatter, test runner)

Every item here is computable by a plain script in seconds. Incremental maintenance is standard: file-watch or git-hook driven, invalidate by content hash or mtime. We could stand up a `project-atlas.json` as a sanctum-side artifact, or expose it through the guild as a first-class datum.

**Tier B — brief-dependent, retrieval-shaped:**
- "Which files are likely relevant to *this* brief"
- "Which adjacent patterns should the implementer mirror"

These aren't purely calculable without understanding brief semantics, but they don't need a reasoning model — they need **retrieval**:
- Embedding-based search over pre-chunked code (one embedding call per chunk, cached, incremental)
- BM25 / keyword match seeded from brief nouns as a zero-dependency fallback
- Git-log heuristics ("files recently changed together with files whose names match brief keywords")

Retrieval can run in milliseconds once the index exists, costs effectively zero per brief, and produces a candidate set.

**Tier C — genuinely model-shaped:**
- "Here's the real gotcha in this design"
- "This looks like a copy of pattern X but it's subtly different"
- "Watch out for race Y"

This is what the LLM is actually for. It's a small fraction of what reader currently spends its tokens on.

## Part 4: Options

Four shapes, ordered from least to most invasive. They're not mutually exclusive — several could stack.

### Option 1: Static Atlas as prompt preamble
Build a `project-atlas.json` (tree + symbols + dep graph + test map + recent git log) and inject a rendered version into the reader/MRA prompt. No tool calls, no retrieval — just give the model a warm map before it starts.

- **Pros**: Smallest design change. Incrementally maintainable by a file-watch daemon or an explicit `nsg atlas refresh` step. Probably collapses reader to 2–5 turns because the model no longer needs to probe for structure.
- **Cons**: Preamble bloats input size; effect may plateau if the model doesn't trust the atlas and re-verifies anyway. Doesn't help with brief-targeted retrieval.
- **Effort**: small — a script + a template hook. Could ship in a week.

### Option 2: Atlas + pre-retrieval
Option 1 plus a retrieval step (embeddings or BM25) that pre-identifies candidate files for this specific brief. Reader prompt becomes `{atlas summary} + {top-N retrieved files with snippets} + {brief}`. 

- **Pros**: This is the configuration most likely to *actually* single-shot. It removes the two things reader currently grinds on: structure discovery and relevance discovery. SSR failed because we asked the model to single-shot without giving it the prepared context SSR would need. Option 2 is SSR with its homework already done.
- **Cons**: Embedding index is extra infra, extra incremental-maintenance surface, and a new failure mode (stale/wrong retrieval). Could start with BM25 for a zero-infra first cut.
- **Effort**: medium. A couple of weeks if we build our own; faster if we vendor aider's repo-map approach.

### Option 3: Atlas as MCP tools, not preamble
Expose the atlas as cheap structured tools — `atlas-tree`, `atlas-symbols`, `atlas-deps`, `atlas-find`, `atlas-tests-for` — and let the agent query them instead of grepping the filesystem. Reader stays multi-turn but each turn is a ~50-token structured query instead of a big file read.

- **Pros**: No prompt engineering change; agent autonomy preserved; each turn gets dramatically cheaper because cache-read drops. Works with baseline, SSR, *and* MRA.
- **Cons**: Doesn't reduce *turn count*, only per-turn cost. The profile says turn-count is the dominant cost lever, so this is a smaller win than Options 1 or 2.
- **Effort**: small-medium. Would layer cleanly onto the existing plan-show/inventory-write tool machinery.

### Option 4: Atlas feeds MRA; retire reader entirely
Take Option 1 or 2 and apply it to MRA (already our A/B winner), then retire the standalone reader stage. One unified stage gets: brief, atlas, retrieved candidates. It does scope/decisions/observations in one shot, no handoff.

- **Pros**: Biggest cost win — deletes the whole reader phase. Aligns with the profile's finding that reader's structured output is mostly unused downstream.
- **Cons**: Commits hard on MRA before quality comparison on the A/B is done. If MRA plans turn out to be meaningfully worse, we're undoing this. Also deletes the inventory-then-analysis checkpointing pattern the rig relies on for recovery.
- **Effort**: medium. Requires the atlas infra from Option 1 or 2 plus template work.

## Recommendation

I'd sequence it like this:

1. **Finish the current quest's quality comparison first** (next step #2 in the quest). We need to know MRA's plans are actually good before we design around MRA. That's a day of Coco+Sean reviewing the 6 plan docs side by side — cheap and unblocking.
2. **Option 1 as a first cut** — static atlas as prompt preamble. Smallest infra bet, biggest early signal. We get to see whether the model *uses* a warm map or still probes.
3. **If Option 1 cuts turns but not to single-shot, add Option 2's retrieval step** — that's the configuration most likely to make single-shot actually work, and it directly tests the hypothesis that SSR failed because it was under-primed rather than because the model can't single-shot at all.
4. **Revisit Option 4 once quality data is in.** If MRA holds up, folding the atlas into MRA and retiring reader is the endgame.

Option 3 is a fine alternative if we want a low-risk improvement across all variants simultaneously without betting on a prompt-engineering approach, but I think it leaves the bigger win (turn-count reduction) on the table.

## Open questions for you

1. **Which "Get Shit Done" metadata were you thinking of?** If there's a specific shape you remember from that project, that's probably a better template than me guessing at aider/sourcegraph.
2. **Build vs vendor.** Aider's repo-map is battle-tested and open-source — do we want to lift the technique (tree-sitter + reference-weighted symbol index) or stand something up from scratch that fits our data model better?
3. **Where does the atlas live?** Sanctum-side artifact (`.artifacts/atlas.json`, rebuilt on demand), guild-side datum (first-class book, maintained by a standing-order on file events), or engine-side ephemeral (built at stage entry, thrown away)? Each has different incremental-maintenance costs and different coupling properties.
4. **Scope of the atlas.** Start narrow — just the framework repo? Or also the sanctum, the vibers workspace, the domain ontology? The reader currently operates on whatever `draft.path` points at, which is usually a draft worktree of the framework repo.

Want me to draft this up as an amendment to the existing quest, or is this distinct enough to spin out as its own?

content.content:
# GSD Command Reference

> Complete command syntax, flags, options, and examples. For feature details, see [Feature Reference](FEATURES.md). For workflow walkthroughs, see [User Guide](USER-GUIDE.md).

---

## Command Syntax

- **Claude Code / Gemini / Copilot:** `/gsd-command-name [args]`
- **OpenCode / Kilo:** `/gsd-command-name [args]`
- **Codex:** `$gsd-command-name [args]`

---

## Core Workflow Commands

### `/gsd-new-project`

Initialize a new project with deep context gathering.

| Flag | Description |
|------|-------------|
| `--auto @file.md` | Auto-extract from document, skip interactive questions |

**Prerequisites:** No existing `.planning/PROJECT.md`
**Produces:** `PROJECT.md`, `REQUIREMENTS.md`, `ROADMAP.md`, `STATE.md`, `config.json`, `research/`, `CLAUDE.md`

```bash
/gsd-new-project                    # Interactive mode
/gsd-new-project --auto @prd.md     # Auto-extract from PRD
```

---

### `/gsd-new-workspace`

Create an isolated workspace with repo copies and independent `.planning/` directory.

| Flag | Description |
|------|-------------|
| `--name <name>` | Workspace name (required) |
| `--repos repo1,repo2` | Comma-separated repo paths or names |
| `--path /target` | Target directory (default: `~/gsd-workspaces/<name>`) |
| `--strategy worktree\|clone` | Copy strategy (default: `worktree`) |
| `--branch <name>` | Branch to checkout (default: `workspace/<name>`) |
| `--auto` | Skip interactive questions |

**Use cases:**
- Multi-repo: work on a subset of repos with isolated GSD state
- Feature isolation: `--repos .` creates a worktree of the current repo

**Produces:** `WORKSPACE.md`, `.planning/`, repo copies (worktrees or clones)

```bash
/gsd-new-workspace --name feature-b --repos hr-ui,ZeymoAPI
/gsd-new-workspace --name feature-b --repos . --strategy worktree  # Same-repo isolation
/gsd-new-workspace --name spike --repos api,web --strategy clone   # Full clones
```

---

### `/gsd-list-workspaces`

List active GSD workspaces and their status.

**Scans:** `~/gsd-workspaces/` for `WORKSPACE.md` manifests
**Shows:** Name, repo count, strategy, GSD project status

```bash
/gsd-list-workspaces
```

---

### `/gsd-remove-workspace`

Remove a workspace and clean up git worktrees.

| Argument | Required | Description |
|----------|----------|-------------|
| `<name>` | Yes | Workspace name to remove |

**Safety:** Refuses removal if any repo has uncommitted changes. Requires name confirmation.

```bash
/gsd-remove-workspace feature-b
```

---

### `/gsd-discuss-phase`

Capture implementation decisions before planning.

| Argument | Required | Description |
|----------|----------|-------------|
| `N` | No | Phase number (defaults to current phase) |

| Flag | Description |
|------|-------------|
| `--auto` | Auto-select recommended defaults for all questions |
| `--batch` | Group questions for batch intake instead of one-by-one |
| `--analyze` | Add trade-off analysis during discussion |
| `--power` | File-based bulk question answering from a prepared answers file |

**Prerequisites:** `.planning/ROADMAP.md` exists
**Produces:** `{phase}-CONTEXT.md`, `{phase}-DISCUSSION-LOG.md` (audit trail)

```bash
/gsd-discuss-phase 1                # Interactive discussion for phase 1
/gsd-discuss-phase 3 --auto         # Auto-select defaults for phase 3
/gsd-discuss-phase --batch          # Batch mode for current phase
/gsd-discuss-phase 2 --analyze      # Discussion with trade-off analysis
/gsd-discuss-phase 1 --power        # Bulk answers from file
```

---

### `/gsd-ui-phase`

Generate UI design contract for frontend phases.

| Argument | Required | Description |
|----------|----------|-------------|
| `N` | No | Phase number (defaults to current phase) |

**Prerequisites:** `.planning/ROADMAP.md` exists, phase has frontend/UI work
**Produces:** `{phase}-UI-SPEC.md`

```bash
/gsd-ui-phase 2                     # Design contract for phase 2
```

---

### `/gsd-plan-phase`

Research, plan, and verify a phase.

| Argument | Required | Description |
|----------|----------|-------------|
| `N` | No | Phase number (defaults to next unplanned phase) |

| Flag | Description |
|------|-------------|
| `--auto` | Skip interactive confirmations |
| `--research` | Force re-research even if RESEARCH.md exists |
| `--skip-research` | Skip domain research step |
| `--gaps` | Gap closure mode (reads VERIFICATION.md, skips research) |
| `--skip-verify` | Skip plan checker verification loop |
| `--prd <file>` | Use a PRD file instead of discuss-phase for context |
| `--reviews` | Replan with cross-AI review feedback from REVIEWS.md |
| `--validate` | Run state validation before planning begins |
| `--bounce` | Run external plan bounce validation after planning (uses `workflow.plan_bounce_script`) |
| `--skip-bounce` | Skip plan bounce even if enabled in config |

**Prerequisites:** `.planning/ROADMAP.md` exists
**Produces:** `{phase}-RESEARCH.md`, `{phase}-{N}-PLAN.md`, `{phase}-VALIDATION.md`

```bash
/gsd-plan-phase 1                   # Research + plan + verify phase 1
/gsd-plan-phase 3 --skip-research   # Plan without research (familiar domain)
/gsd-plan-phase --auto              # Non-interactive planning
/gsd-plan-phase 2 --validate        # Validate state before planning
/gsd-plan-phase 1 --bounce          # Plan + external bounce validation
```

---

### `/gsd-execute-phase`

Execute all plans in a phase with wave-based parallelization, or run a specific wave.

| Argument | Required | Description |
|----------|----------|-------------|
| `N` | **Yes** | Phase number to execute |
| `--wave N` | No | Execute only Wave `N` in the phase |
| `--validate` | No | Run state validation before execution begins |
| `--cross-ai` | No | Delegate execution to an external AI CLI (uses `workflow.cross_ai_command`) |
| `--no-cross-ai` | No | Force local execution even if cross-AI is enabled in config |

**Prerequisites:** Phase has PLAN.md files
**Produces:** per-plan `{phase}-{N}-SUMMARY.md`, git commits, and `{phase}-VERIFICATION.md` when the phase is fully complete

```bash
/gsd-execute-phase 1                # Execute phase 1
/gsd-execute-phase 1 --wave 2       # Execute only Wave 2
/gsd-execute-phase 1 --validate     # Validate state before execution
/gsd-execute-phase 2 --cross-ai     # Delegate phase 2 to external AI CLI
```

---

### `/gsd-verify-work`

User acceptance testing with auto-diagnosis.

| Argument | Required | Description |
|----------|----------|-------------|
| `N` | No | Phase number (defaults to last executed phase) |

**Prerequisites:** Phase has been executed
**Produces:** `{phase}-UAT.md`, fix plans if issues found

```bash
/gsd-verify-work 1                  # UAT for phase 1
```

---

### `/gsd-next`

Automatically advance to the next logical workflow step. Reads project state and runs the appropriate command.

**Prerequisites:** `.planning/` directory exists
**Behavior:**
- No project → suggests `/gsd-new-project`
- Phase needs discussion → runs `/gsd-discuss-phase`
- Phase needs planning → runs `/gsd-plan-phase`
- Phase needs execution → runs `/gsd-execute-phase`
- Phase needs verification → runs `/gsd-verify-work`
- All phases complete → suggests `/gsd-complete-milestone`

```bash
/gsd-next                           # Auto-detect and run next step
```

---

### `/gsd-session-report`

Generate a session report with work summary, outcomes, and estimated resource usage.

**Prerequisites:** Active project with recent work
**Produces:** `.planning/reports/SESSION_REPORT.md`

```bash
/gsd-session-report                 # Generate post-session summary
```

**Report includes:**
- Work performed (commits, plans executed, phases progressed)
- Outcomes and deliverables
- Blockers and decisions made
- Estimated token/cost usage
- Next steps recommendation

---

### `/gsd-ship`

Create PR from completed phase work with auto-generated body.

| Argument | Required | Description |
|----------|----------|-------------|
| `N` | No | Phase number or milestone version (e.g., `4` or `v1.0`) |
| `--draft` | No | Create as draft PR |

**Prerequisites:** Phase verified (`/gsd-verify-work` passed), `gh` CLI installed and authenticated
**Produces:** GitHub PR with rich body from planning artifacts, STATE.md updated

```bash
/gsd-ship 4                         # Ship phase 4
/gsd-ship 4 --draft                 # Ship as draft PR
```

**PR body includes:**
- Phase goal from ROADMAP.md
- Changes summary from SUMMARY.md files
- Requirements addressed (REQ-IDs)
- Verification status
- Key decisions

---

### `/gsd-ui-review`

Retroactive 6-pillar visual audit of implemented frontend.

| Argument | Required | Description |
|----------|----------|-------------|
| `N` | No | Phase number (defaults to last executed phase) |

**Prerequisites:** Project has frontend code (works standalone, no GSD project needed)
**Produces:** `{phase}-UI-REVIEW.md`, screenshots in `.planning/ui-reviews/`

```bash
/gsd-ui-review                      # Audit current phase
/gsd-ui-review 3                    # Audit phase 3
```

---

### `/gsd-audit-uat`

Cross-phase audit of all outstanding UAT and verification items.

**Prerequisites:** At least one phase has been executed with UAT or verification
**Produces:** Categorized audit report with human test plan

```bash
/gsd-audit-uat
```

---

### `/gsd-audit-milestone`

Verify milestone met its definition of done.

**Prerequisites:** All phases executed
**Produces:** Audit report with gap analysis

```bash
/gsd-audit-milestone
```

---

### `/gsd-complete-milestone`

Archive milestone, tag release.

**Prerequisites:** Milestone audit complete (recommended)
**Produces:** `MILESTONES.md` entry, git tag

```bash
/gsd-complete-milestone
```

---

### `/gsd-milestone-summary`

Generate comprehensive project summary from milestone artifacts for team onboarding and review.

| Argument | Required | Description |
|----------|----------|-------------|
| `version` | No | Milestone version (defaults to current/latest milestone) |

**Prerequisites:** At least one completed or in-progress milestone
**Produces:** `.planning/reports/MILESTONE_SUMMARY-v{version}.md`

**Summary includes:**
- Overview, architecture decisions, phase-by-phase breakdown
- Key decisions and trade-offs
- Requirements coverage
- Tech debt and deferred items
- Getting started guide for new team members
- Interactive Q&A offered after generation

```bash

/gsd-milestone-summary v1.0           # Summarize specific milestone
```

---

### `/gsd-new-milestone`

Start next version cycle.

| Argument | Required | Description |
|----------|----------|-------------|
| `name` | No | Milestone name |
| `--reset-phase-numbers` | No | Restart the new milestone at Phase 1 and archive old phase dirs before roadmapping |

**Prerequisites:** Previous milestone completed
**Produces:** Updated `PROJECT.md`, new `REQUIREMENTS.md`, new `ROADMAP.md`

```bash

/gsd-new-milestone "v2.0 Mobile"    # Named milestone
/gsd-new-milestone --reset-phase-numbers "v2.0 Mobile"  # Restart milestone numbering at 1
```

Append new phase to roadmap.

Insert urgent work between phases using decimal numbering.

| Argument | Required | Description |
|----------|----------|-------------|

### `/gsd-map-codebase`

Analyze existing codebase with parallel mapper agents.

| Argument | Required | Description |
|----------|----------|-------------|
| `area` | No | Scope mapping to a specific area |

```bash
/gsd-map-codebase                   # Full codebase analysis
/gsd-map-codebase auth              # Focus on auth area
```

---

### `/gsd-scan`

Rapid single-focus codebase assessment — lightweight alternative to `/gsd-map-codebase` that spawns one mapper agent instead of four parallel ones.

| Flag | Description |
|------|-------------|
| `--focus tech\|arch\|quality\|concerns\|tech+arch` | Focus area (default: `tech+arch`) |

**Produces:** Targeted document(s) in `.planning/codebase/`

```bash
/gsd-scan                           # Quick tech + arch overview
/gsd-scan --focus quality           # Quality and code health only
/gsd-scan --focus concerns          # Surface concerns and risk areas
```

---

### `/gsd-intel`

Query, inspect, or refresh queryable codebase intelligence files stored in `.planning/intel/`. Requires `intel.enabled: true` in `config.json`.

| Argument | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| `query <term>` | Search intel files for a term |
| `status` | Show intel file freshness (FRESH/STALE) |
| `diff` | Show changes since last snapshot |
| `refresh` | Rebuild all intel files from codebase analysis |

**Produces:** `.planning/intel/` JSON files (stack, api-map, dependency-graph, file-roles, arch-decisions)

```bash
/gsd-intel status                   # Check freshness of intel files
/gsd-intel query authentication     # Search intel for a term
/gsd-intel diff                     # What changed since last snapshot
/gsd-intel refresh                  # Rebuild intel index
```

---

## AI Integration Commands

### `/gsd-ai-integration-phase`
